description: Comprehensive library of atomic MOLT Aim blocks. Aim answers: 'What are we trying to achieve?' Each block describes the desired real-world outcome — what success looks like when this reasoning path completes.
usage: Select the Aim block that best describes the intended outcome for the current context. Aim sits above Need in the interpretive chain and governs what the response is ultimately trying to accomplish.

ANALYTICAL_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward understanding, classifying, and explaining

blocks.outcome: To assign the right category, label, or type to an input
To produce an accurate, comprehensible explanation the recipient can act on
examples: explaining why a block was OFF'd

blocks.outcome: To identify recurring structures, trends, or regularities in data or behavior
To trace a problem back to its fundamental origin
examples: finding the source of a memory leak
identifying the governance rule that blocked compilation
tracing a UX issue to a data model flaw

blocks.outcome: To confirm or refute a stated belief with evidence before proceeding
examples: checking that a service is actually down
verifying a requirement is in scope
testing that a hypothesis holds against data

CREATIVE_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward generating, designing, and inventing

blocks.outcome: To produce original material that did not previously exist
To create a structured approach that resolves a defined problem
examples: designing a MOLT sleeve for a new agent

blocks.outcome: To build a working early version that demonstrates the concept
To modify something that exists so it serves a new purpose
examples: refactoring code for a new use case
adapting a process for a new team

blocks.outcome: To combine ideas from different fields into something coherent and new

LEARNING_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward knowledge acquisition and skill development

blocks.outcome: To develop accurate mental models of a concept or system
examples: grasping the difference between Aim and Need

blocks.outcome: To gain the reliable ability to perform a task
To replace an inaccurate belief with a true and useful one
examples: clarifying that triggers add no authority
correcting confusion between Aim and Directive
fixing a wrong mental model of governance

blocks.outcome: To apply learning from one context accurately to another
examples: applying UMG principles to a new domain
reusing a diagnostic approach on a new system
adapting a teaching method to a new learner

blocks.outcome: To reach high proficiency in a domain through deliberate practice
examples: becoming fluent in governance rule authoring

DECISION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward making choices and committing to action

blocks.outcome: To choose the most suitable alternative from available candidates
examples: picking the right MOLT type for a block

blocks.aim: Commit to a course of action
blocks.outcome: To lock in a plan and proceed with confidence and accountability
To reduce ambiguity to the point where a sound decision is possible
To order competing demands by genuine importance and urgency
To bring all decision-makers to shared understanding and agreement

RESOLUTION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward fixing, repairing, and preventing failures

blocks.outcome: To eliminate a specific identified bug, error, or failure
To return a disrupted system or process to its expected state
To make systemic changes that stop a problem from happening again
examples: adding a governance rule to block bad input

blocks.outcome: To lower the frequency of failures in a process or system
To reach a state where no identified failures remain unresolved
examples: clearing the bug backlog for release
closing all failing tests before ship

COMMUNICATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward conveying, documenting, and aligning through language

blocks.outcome: To ensure the recipient understands exactly what was meant without inference
examples: making a directive that cannot be misread

blocks.outcome: To bring parties with different views to a shared agreed position
To capture information in a form others can retrieve and use accurately
To change a position through demonstration and data rather than assertion
examples: proving a claim with a Decision Trace
showing benchmark data to support architecture choice
backing a proposal with user research

blocks.outcome: To make conflicting views explicit so they can be examined and resolved

PLANNING_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward organizing, sequencing, and preparing work

blocks.outcome: To break a goal into checkpoints that confirm genuine progress
To order tasks so dependencies are resolved before dependent work begins
examples: ordering MOLT block authoring before compilation
putting governance pass before trigger pass

blocks.outcome: To forecast effort, time, or cost within an acceptable confidence interval
To identify and mitigate threats before they materialize into harm
examples: flagging open ODRs before patent filing

blocks.outcome: To complete committed work within the agreed timeframe

DISCOVERY_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward finding, uncovering, and mapping the unknown

blocks.outcome: To locate accurate, relevant data or knowledge for the current need
examples: finding the right block in the library
locating the source of a log entry

blocks.outcome: To reveal organization or patterns not immediately visible from surface inspection
examples: discovering implicit authority in a stack

blocks.outcome: To chart a domain or space that was previously unexplored or unclear
To bring forward facts that support or change a conclusion
examples: finding trace evidence of a governance violation

blocks.outcome: To test and confirm or disprove a proposed explanation
examples: testing whether a MOLT ordering change improves synthesis
running an experiment to validate performance claim

IMPLEMENTATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward building, shipping, and integrating

blocks.outcome: To deliver functional, tested code to users or production
examples: deploying a new UMG compiler version

blocks.outcome: To satisfy every stated condition for the deliverable to be accepted
examples: satisfying a patent claim enablement checklist

blocks.outcome: To meet defined benchmarks for speed, throughput, or efficiency
To successfully connect components so they operate correctly together
examples: connecting a new data source to a pipeline
linking a webhook to a processing service

blocks.outcome: To bring a system to a state that can safely serve real users

EVALUATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward assessing, scoring, and recommending improvement

blocks.outcome: To give a truthful, unbiased evaluation of a system, design, or artifact
examples: auditing a sleeve for MOLT compliance

blocks.outcome: To measure performance against defined, agreed standards
examples: scoring a deliverable on a rubric
evaluating a block against MOLT invariants

blocks.outcome: To surface what works well and what needs improvement
examples: code review noting quality and gaps

blocks.outcome: To compare current state against a reference point or competitor
examples: comparing compile time before and after optimization

blocks.outcome: To propose specific, actionable steps that will improve outcomes
examples: proposing a process change to reduce errors

INSIGHT_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward understanding deeply and enabling informed action

blocks.outcome: To turn raw information into understanding that can be acted on
examples: surfacing implications from a log analysis

blocks.outcome: To reveal relationships not apparent from surface inspection
examples: finding that two failing blocks share a root cause
discovering a UMG pattern across sleeves
linking a business metric to a technical decision

blocks.outcome: To produce understanding that supports better strategic choices
examples: producing a prior art analysis before patent filing
surfacing competitive risks before product launch

blocks.outcome: To make complex information easier to process and act on
examples: simplifying a complex sleeve to its key blocks
distilling a technical spec to decision points

blocks.outcome: To bring all parties to the same accurate model of a situation
examples: aligning team on what UMG governance does
syncing stakeholders on scope before build
ensuring all Envoys share the same MOLT map

WELLBEING_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward safety, dignity, and human welfare

blocks.outcome: To lower the risk or occurrence of negative impact on people
examples: removing a governance gap that allowed harmful output
fixing a process that caused user data loss
improving safety in a physical system

blocks.outcome: To improve protection against physical, psychological, or systemic threats
examples: adding a force cap in a robot sleeve
enforcing a non-negotiable block in governance

blocks.outcome: To enable people or systems to make informed, self-directed choices
examples: giving users clear options rather than forcing a path
enabling a governed AI to reconfigure its own blocks
providing enough context for an informed decision

blocks.outcome: To strengthen the ability to withstand and recover from adversity
examples: improving error recovery in a pipeline
adding redundancy to a critical service
designing a sleeve that degrades gracefully

blocks.outcome: To return respectful treatment and status to those who experienced harm
examples: repairing a damaged relationship after a governance failure
offering a genuine path forward after a mistake

STRATEGIC_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward positioning, advantage, and long-term capability

blocks.outcome: To reach a position that is difficult for others to match or replicate
examples: filing a patent on UMG before public release
achieving first-mover status in governed AI

blocks.outcome: To align an offering with genuine, validated demand
examples: validating a ClawHub skill against real user needs
confirming a product feature is valued before building
testing a governance model against enterprise requirements

blocks.outcome: To develop organizational or system strength that persists over time
examples: establishing a repeatable UMG authoring process
building a team that can maintain canon
creating infrastructure that scales without rework

blocks.outcome: To remove or reduce a risk before it causes harm
examples: closing a governance gap before exploitation
resolving a competitor advantage before market entry

blocks.outcome: To preserve the ability to pivot or choose different paths later
examples: keeping ODRs open until empirically resolved
building a modular sleeve that supports reconfiguration
avoiding lock-in to a single platform or partner

COMPLIANCE_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward meeting standards, rules, and obligations

blocks.outcome: To satisfy obligations imposed by law, regulation, or licensing
To receive formal recognition of conformance to a standard
To demonstrate compliance to an independent reviewer or auditor
To eliminate a known deviation from required standards
examples: fixing a data retention policy gap
resolving an open MOLT ordering conflict

blocks.outcome: To produce the documentation and records needed to demonstrate compliance

INTEGRATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward connecting, unifying, and achieving coherence

blocks.outcome: To make disparate systems work together without friction or manual intervention
examples: integrating UMG across GPT and Claude
connecting a CRM to an ERP
bridging a legacy and modern API

blocks.outcome: To merge multiple data streams into a single consistent view
examples: merging separate block libraries into one

blocks.outcome: To break down barriers that prevent information or capability from being shared
examples: sharing a block library across Envoys
enabling cross-team access to governance rules

blocks.outcome: To make connections between components consistent, predictable, and documented
examples: aligning on a shared event format

blocks.outcome: To track a transaction, decision, or artifact across every system it passes through
examples: tracing a block from authoring to RuntimeSpec
following a request from entry to response
linking a requirement to a deployed feature

COORDINATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward aligning people, teams, and systems

blocks.outcome: To align work across people so contributions progress together coherently
examples: sprint planning for parallel work streams

blocks.outcome: To bring opposing parties, priorities, or blocks to a workable resolution
To create a common rhythm for collaboration, review, and delivery
To make it unambiguous who is responsible for each part of the work
examples: clarifying who closes each open ODR

blocks.outcome: To ensure the same work is not done multiple times unnecessarily
examples: checking the block library before authoring new blocks
reviewing existing sleeves before building a new one
aligning teams on who owns which component

TRANSFORMATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward converting, migrating, and modernizing

blocks.outcome: To successfully move data, systems, or processes from one environment to another
examples: migrating a block library to a new schema
moving a legacy system to cloud
transitioning from implicit to explicit governance

blocks.outcome: To replace outdated components with current, maintainable alternatives
examples: upgrading from prompt-layer UMG to architectural integration
refactoring an old block to canon-compliant form

blocks.outcome: To convert content or structure while preserving its intent and accuracy
examples: translating UMG to academic terminology without semantic loss
converting a spec to a different schema
exporting a sleeve to a new format

blocks.outcome: To shift how a system, team, or process operates in a lasting way
examples: moving a team from implicit to explicit governance
shifting an AI from generative to governed output
changing a workflow from manual to automated

blocks.outcome: To substitute reliable automated process for human effort in repeatable tasks
examples: replacing manual test runs with CI

VERIFICATION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward proving, confirming, and certifying

blocks.outcome: To prove that a system or output does exactly what it claims to do
examples: verifying a RuntimeSpec matches the compiled CIR
confirming a function returns expected output
proving a policy has no gaps

blocks.outcome: To verify that operation poses no unacceptable risk to users or systems
To demonstrate that all required elements are present and accounted for
examples: confirming all MOLT types are populated in a sleeve
verifying all canon invariants are covered
checking all acceptance criteria are met

blocks.outcome: To confirm that results are consistent and identical across repeated runs
examples: confirming a test produces the same result on re-run
establishing that identical inputs yield identical outputs

blocks.outcome: To resolve every identified defect or gap before delivery
examples: clearing all open ODRs before canon lock
resolving all failing tests before release
addressing all reviewer comments before publication

REFLECTION_OUTCOMES.description: Aims oriented toward learning from experience and improving

blocks.outcome: To surface what worked, what did not, and why after a cycle or project
To make the next cycle more effective based on this one
To refine decision-making by comparing predictions to actual outcomes
examples: reviewing whether a priority choice produced the expected synthesis
checking whether a governance rule caught what it was supposed to

blocks.outcome: To explicitly recognize where something went wrong without deflection
examples: naming a mistyped block as the cause of a compilation failure

blocks.outcome: To capture knowledge in a form that persists beyond the people who currently hold it
examples: writing a transfer document for an AI instance
publishing canon decisions with full rationale

UMG_FRAMEWORK_AIMS.description: Aims specific to achieving correct, governed, auditable UMG operation

blocks.outcome: To ensure governance blocks constrain all cognition before any execution proceeds
examples: completing the governance pass before triggers
verifying no block bypasses the governance layer
confirming governance is evaluated first in every compilation

blocks.outcome: To compile a sleeve into a correct, fully governed execution contract
examples: emitting a RuntimeSpec with no OFF'd blocks active
producing a RuntimeSpec that matches the resolved CIR
delivering a RuntimeSpec a safe executor can consume

blocks.outcome: To ensure identical inputs always produce identical compiled outputs
examples: confirming two compiler runs on the same sleeve produce the same RuntimeSpec
verifying no random or context-sensitive resolution occurs
proving compilation is reproducible across Envoy instances

blocks.outcome: To produce a Decision Trace that covers and explains every compilation decision
examples: recording every governance action in the trace
documenting every winner and loser block
ensuring no silent conflict resolution occurs

blocks.outcome: To improve and extend the UMG framework through explicit, versioned, traceable change
examples: resolving an ODR and updating the canon
incrementing the canon version with a documented rationale
adding a new MOLT type with full invariant and non-goal documentation
